narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 36, lower-middle family income, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at 2—meaning you placed in the lower end of your class compared to typical students, whose averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 56.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment drawing from thousands of similar student outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the model's view toward expecting you to pass since it's close to the average of 36.7 seen in successful students. However, your Year 3 decile ranking carried the most significant weight in the opposite direction, strongly signaling a higher risk of failure because it was well below the typical 5.53 average, suggesting challenges in sustaining academic performance later in school. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely as the third key factor, also nudging the prediction toward failure risk due to its 2.50 level compared to the 3.21 average among bar passers. Your plan to attend full-time, like over 90% of students, and your family income didn't sway things much in this case. Overall, while your LSAT held you back from a worse outcome, the combination of your lower Year 3 decile and undergrad GPA outweighed it, placing your profile among those historically more likely to struggle on the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your recent academic record through postgraduate courses or a master's program to improve those decile rankings. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience as a Hispanic student from a lower-middle income background could also help in reapplying, perhaps to schools with holistic reviews. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your next application could tell a story of growth that shifts the odds. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen for determined applicants like you.
